pi_items.verified_value: mroz dataset from PSID 1975 labor supply data
pi_items.source: Wooldridge R package, POE5, gretl standard datasets; original source: T.A. Mroz (1987) 'The Sensitivity of an Empirical Model of Married Women's Hours of Work to Economic and Statistical Assumptions,' Econometrica 55, 765-799
http://www.principlesofeconometrics.com/poe5/data/def/mroz.def; the first 428 of 753 total observations represent married women with positive hours worked in 1975
https://solomonegash.com/econometrics/wooldridge_python/iexample15_py.html (Example 15.5: TSLS with mothereduc and fathereduc as instruments yields coefficient 0.0614 with standard error 0.0332)
https://solomonegash.com/econometrics/wooldridge_python/iexample15_py.html and multiple econometrics textbook examples using the mroz dataset
Standard specification for TSLS estimation of returns to education in the mroz dataset, found in multiple econometrics examples and textbooks
The mroz dataset is a standard dataset for teaching TSLS/IV estimation in econometrics; widely used in Wooldridge textbooks and examples
Education is a key variable in the mroz dataset used to study returns to education on wages

privileged_info_summary: The mroz dataset is a real, publicly available dataset containing 753 observations of married women from the Panel Study of Income Dynamics (PSID) 1975, with the first 428 observations representing women with positive hours worked in 1975. The dataset was created by T.A. Mroz (1987) for studying labor supply decisions and is widely used in econometrics education. TSLS estimation with mothereduc and fathereduc as instrumental variables for the endogenous education variable yields an estimated coefficient of 0.0614 (claimed: 0.061) on education (educ) for log wages (lwage), with standard regressors of const, educ, exper, and expersq. This coefficient is significantly smaller than the OLS estimate of approximately 0.107, reflecting the endogeneity bias when education is not instrumented.
